title: The forensic accountant and the heir
summary: A forensic accountant has finished her audit of a recently-deceased uncle's estate. The heir wants to know whether the truth in the books should stay in the books.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: Thank you for coming on a Saturday.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: It is the only quiet day. Where would you like to begin?

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: With the question that is keeping me up. Is the report ready?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: It is. Three hundred and fourteen pages. You can have it on Monday. Before you do, decide what you want from me.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: I want the truth. I am the executor.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: You will get the truth either way. There is a version that contains everything I have found, and a version that contains everything I am required by law and by professional standards to disclose. They are not the same document. The difference is what I am asking you about.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: Is the difference large?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: It is small in pages and large in consequence. There are four entries, between 1991 and 2003, that suggest your uncle helped a former employee through a financial difficulty in a way the tax authority might still treat as undeclared income. He is dead. He paid tax on most of his life. The total amount is not large. The employee, who is now sixty-eight, lives in the apartment your uncle helped her buy. She raised two children there.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: Why do I have to know this?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Because you are the executor, and because in three months you will be asked, under oath, whether the financial picture I have presented is complete. If the omission stays, you will, in a strict reading, not have answered honestly.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: What is the harm if it goes in?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: The estate will probably owe back tax with interest. The woman in the apartment may be subpoenaed to testify about what your uncle paid her and why. Whatever she has built around the apartment becomes part of a public record. Your uncle's name appears in a paragraph that summarises his life as a tax case.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: And if it stays out?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Then you will sign a document that omits something material. The omission is small. The omission is also a lie, in a technical sense. If anyone ever audits the audit, both of our reputations are damaged, and yours legally as well.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: What do you usually advise?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: In ninety per cent of cases, I advise full disclosure. It is the cleanest position. The remaining ten per cent are like this one. They are cases where disclosure produces a real harm to a third party that the disclosure itself does not remedy. The state collects back tax that does not bring your uncle back; an old woman loses peace; nobody is repaired.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: So you are saying I should bury the four entries.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: No. I am saying that whichever choice you make is a moral one, and that the form of the report cannot do that work for you. Most clients in your position want me to make the choice. I do not, on principle. I will not put my name on a sealed version unless you have understood, in writing, what is sealed and what your duties as executor are.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: May I ask what you would do, if you were me?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: You may, but I cannot answer cleanly. I will tell you what I have learned. Full disclosure is almost always defensible and almost always proportionate. The harder position is defending non-disclosure when the harm is small, distant, and recoverable, and the third party cannot defend themselves. There is a coherent ethical position for that choice. It is not the one the law assumes you will take. It is the position you have to be ready to defend, by yourself, for the rest of your life, if it is ever opened.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: What do most clients choose?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Full disclosure. Mostly because they want to sleep. A small minority bury, and of those, a smaller minority can defend the choice ten years later. The ones who can defend it later usually insisted, at the time, on understanding exactly what they were burying and whom they were protecting. They did not pretend they had no choice. They chose, and they remembered the choosing.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: Linh, I think I want to talk to the woman in the apartment first.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: That is a better instinct than either choice. She is in some sense the affected party. She is also the person whose right to know is least often considered.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: Will you be at the meeting?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: If you want me there. I will not speak. But I will witness what you tell her and what you ask of her. That keeps me on the right side of the line. And it makes whatever you decide afterwards harder to forget.

body.speaker: Bao
body.text: Set it up for next week.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: I will. One last thing. Whatever you decide, you should decide in the meeting, not before it. People who walk in with a decision usually defend it. People who walk in with a question usually hear the answer.

annotations.meaningVi: bỏ sót một điều có tính trọng yếu (về pháp lý)
có thể khắc phục / hoàn trả lại được

grammarNotes.title: Layered modals for cautious professional speech
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Linh dùng các lớp modal — 'might', 'may', 'will probably', 'is likely to' — để tách bạch xác suất pháp lý với mệnh lệnh đạo đức. Trong bài: 'the tax authority might still treat as undeclared income' (khả năng pháp lý), 'The estate will probably owe back tax' (dự đoán cao), 'You may, but I cannot answer cleanly' (cho phép kèm giới hạn). Cấu trúc này cho phép cô tư vấn mà không quyết định thay cho Bảo.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Linh layers modals to separate legal probability from moral instruction. 'The tax authority might still treat as undeclared income' (low certainty), 'The estate will probably owe back tax' (high certainty), 'You may, but I cannot answer cleanly' (granting permission with limits). The layered modality lets her advise without deciding for him.

translationVi:
Bảo: Cảm ơn cô đã đến vào thứ Bảy.
Linh: Hôm nay là ngày duy nhất yên tĩnh. Anh muốn bắt đầu từ đâu?
Bảo: Từ câu hỏi đang giữ tôi mất ngủ. Báo cáo xong chưa?
Linh: Xong. Ba trăm mười bốn trang. Thứ Hai anh có thể nhận. Trước khi nhận, anh hãy quyết anh muốn gì từ tôi.
Bảo: Tôi muốn sự thật. Tôi là người thi hành di chúc.
Linh: Đằng nào anh cũng sẽ có sự thật. Có một phiên bản chứa mọi thứ tôi đã tìm thấy, và một phiên bản chứa những thứ tôi buộc phải công bố theo luật và tiêu chuẩn nghề nghiệp. Đó không phải cùng một văn bản. Khác biệt là điều tôi đang hỏi anh.
Bảo: Khác biệt có lớn không?
Linh: Nhỏ về số trang, lớn về hệ quả. Có bốn mục, từ 1991 đến 2003, cho thấy bác anh đã giúp một cựu nhân viên vượt qua khó khăn tài chính theo cách mà cơ quan thuế có thể vẫn coi là thu nhập chưa khai báo. Ông đã mất. Ông đã đóng thuế cho phần lớn cuộc đời. Tổng số tiền không lớn. Người nhân viên đó, nay sáu mươi tám tuổi, sống trong căn hộ mà bác anh đã giúp bà mua. Bà nuôi hai đứa con ở đó.
Bảo: Vì sao tôi phải biết chuyện này?
Linh: Vì anh là người thi hành di chúc, và vì trong ba tháng nữa anh sẽ được hỏi, dưới lời tuyên thệ, liệu bức tranh tài chính tôi đã trình bày có đầy đủ không. Nếu sự bỏ sót vẫn còn, thì theo cách đọc nghiêm khắc, anh sẽ không trả lời thành thật.
Bảo: Thiệt hại là gì nếu nó được đưa vào?
Linh: Tài sản có lẽ sẽ phải nộp truy thu thuế cộng lãi. Người phụ nữ trong căn hộ có thể bị triệu tập để khai về việc bác anh đã trả gì cho bà và vì sao. Mọi thứ bà đã xây quanh căn hộ trở thành một phần hồ sơ công. Tên bác anh xuất hiện trong một đoạn tóm lược đời ông như một vụ thuế.
Bảo: Còn nếu nó được giữ ngoài?
Linh: Thì anh sẽ ký một văn bản bỏ sót một điều trọng yếu. Sự bỏ sót nhỏ. Sự bỏ sót cũng là một lời nói dối, theo nghĩa kỹ thuật. Nếu bao giờ có người kiểm toán lại cuộc kiểm toán này, uy tín của cả hai chúng ta bị tổn hại, và của anh thì bị về mặt pháp lý nữa.
Bảo: Cô thường khuyên gì?
Linh: Trong 90% các trường hợp, tôi khuyên công bố đầy đủ. Đó là lập trường sạch nhất. Mười phần trăm còn lại giống vụ này. Đó là những trường hợp công bố tạo ra thiệt hại thực tế cho một bên thứ ba mà bản thân việc công bố không khắc phục được. Nhà nước thu lại truy thu thuế nhưng không đem bác anh trở lại; một bà cụ mất đi sự bình yên; không ai được sửa.
Bảo: Vậy cô đang nói tôi nên chôn bốn mục đó.
Linh: Không. Tôi đang nói rằng dù anh chọn cách nào, đó cũng là một lựa chọn đạo đức, và hình thức của bản báo cáo không thể làm phần việc đó thay anh. Đa số khách hàng ở vị trí của anh muốn tôi quyết hộ. Tôi không làm vậy, vì nguyên tắc. Tôi sẽ không đặt tên tôi lên một bản niêm phong nếu anh chưa hiểu, bằng văn bản, cái gì đang được niêm phong và nghĩa vụ của anh với tư cách người thi hành di chúc là gì.
Bảo: Tôi hỏi: nếu cô là tôi, cô sẽ làm gì?
Linh: Anh có thể hỏi, nhưng tôi không thể trả lời sạch. Tôi sẽ nói với anh điều tôi đã học được. Công bố đầy đủ gần như luôn bảo vệ được và gần như luôn tương xứng. Lập trường khó hơn là bảo vệ việc không công bố khi thiệt hại nhỏ, xa, và có thể phục hồi, và bên thứ ba không thể tự bảo vệ mình. Có một lập trường đạo đức mạch lạc cho lựa chọn đó. Đó không phải lập trường mà luật giả định anh sẽ chọn. Đó là lập trường anh phải sẵn sàng tự bảo vệ, một mình, cho phần đời còn lại của mình, nếu một ngày nào đó nó bị mở ra.
Bảo: Đa số khách hàng chọn gì?
Linh: Công bố đầy đủ. Chủ yếu vì họ muốn ngủ. Một nhóm nhỏ chôn, và trong số đó, một nhóm nhỏ hơn có thể bảo vệ lựa chọn đó mười năm sau. Những người bảo vệ được về sau thường là những người đã khăng khăng, ngay lúc đó, hiểu chính xác cái họ đang chôn và họ đang bảo vệ ai. Họ không giả vờ rằng mình không có lựa chọn. Họ chọn, và họ nhớ việc đã chọn.
Bảo: Linh, tôi nghĩ tôi muốn nói chuyện với bà cụ trong căn hộ trước.
Linh: Đó là một trực giác tốt hơn cả hai lựa chọn. Theo một nghĩa nào đó, bà là bên bị ảnh hưởng. Bà cũng là người mà quyền được biết của bà ít được cân nhắc nhất.
Bảo: Cô có dự cuộc gặp không?
Linh: Nếu anh muốn tôi có mặt. Tôi sẽ không nói. Nhưng tôi sẽ chứng kiến anh nói gì với bà và yêu cầu gì ở bà. Việc đó giữ tôi ở đúng phía của ranh giới. Và nó khiến mọi thứ anh quyết sau đó khó quên hơn.
Bảo: Sắp xếp tuần sau.
Linh: Tôi sẽ. Một điều cuối. Dù anh quyết gì, anh nên quyết trong cuộc gặp, không phải trước đó. Người bước vào với một quyết định thường bảo vệ nó. Người bước vào với một câu hỏi thường nghe được câu trả lời.

questions.prompt: What two versions of the report does Linh describe?
options: A version with everything she has found, and a version with only what she is legally and professionally required to disclose.
A short version and a long version.
An English version and a Vietnamese version.
questions.explanation: 'A version of the report that contains everything I have found, and a version that contains everything I am required by law and by professional standards to disclose.'

questions.prompt: What is the financial discrepancy concerns?
options: Four entries between 1991 and 2003 that suggest the uncle helped a former employee buy an apartment in a way the tax authority might still treat as undeclared income.
questions.explanation: Detailed in Linh's third long turn.

questions.prompt: Why does Bao 'have to know this,' according to Linh?
options: He is the executor and in three months will be asked under oath whether the financial picture is complete.
His uncle wrote a posthumous letter.
questions.explanation: 'You are the executor, and… in three months you will be asked, under oath, whether the financial picture I have presented is complete.'
questions.hint: Linh's reply to 'Why do I have to know this?'

questions.prompt: If the entries are disclosed, what concrete harms does Linh predict?
options: The estate probably owes back tax with interest; the woman may be subpoenaed; the apartment becomes part of a public record; the uncle's name appears in a tax-case paragraph.
questions.explanation: All four are listed in Linh's reply to 'What is the harm if it goes in?'

questions.prompt: If the entries stay out, what are the risks?
options: Bao signs a document that omits something material; both reputations are damaged if anyone audits the audit, and Bao is liable legally as well.
questions.explanation: Stated in Linh's reply to 'And if it stays out?'

questions.prompt: What does Linh usually advise in ninety per cent of cases?
options: Full disclosure — it is the cleanest position.
Refusing to file a report at all.
questions.explanation: 'In ninety per cent of cases, I advise full disclosure. It is the cleanest position.'
questions.hint: Linh's reply to 'What do you usually advise?'

questions.prompt: What characterises the ten per cent of cases that are different?
options: Disclosure produces a real harm to a third party that the disclosure itself does not remedy.
The amounts are too small to bother with.
The case involves a foreign country.
questions.explanation: Exactly the framing she uses for the exceptional 10%.
questions.hint: Linh's same long turn — what defines the harder cases.

questions.prompt: Why does Linh refuse to make the choice for Bao?
options: Whichever choice is made is a moral one, and the form of the report cannot do that work for the executor; she will not put her name on a sealed version unless he has understood, in writing, what is sealed and what his duties are.
She is not allowed to give opinions.
She does not have enough information.
questions.explanation: 'I am saying that whichever choice you make is a moral one, and that the form of the report cannot do that work for you… I will not put my name on a sealed version unless you have understood, in writing…'
questions.hint: Linh's reply to 'So you are saying I should bury the four entries.'

questions.prompt: How does Linh describe the harder ethical position (non-disclosure)?
options: Defensible when the harm is small, distant, and recoverable, and the third party is unable to defend themselves — but the burden of defending it is the client's, alone, for the rest of their life.
Reserved for cases with large sums.
A position the law assumes you will take.
questions.explanation: Stated explicitly in Linh's longer reply to 'What would you do, if you were me?'
questions.hint: Linh's long reply with 'what I have learned.'

questions.prompt: What does Linh notice about the small minority of clients who can defend non-disclosure ten years later?
options: They insisted on understanding, at the time, exactly what they were burying and whom they were protecting; they did not pretend they had no choice.
They were all from the same industry.
questions.explanation: 'The ones who can defend it ten years later are usually the ones who insisted on understanding…'
questions.hint: Linh's reply about what most clients choose.

questions.prompt: What is Bao's instinct at the end of the conversation?
options: To talk to the woman in the apartment first.
To sign the sealed report immediately.
questions.explanation: 'I think I want to talk to the woman in the apartment first.'

questions.prompt: How does Linh react to this instinct?
options: She says it is a better instinct than either choice; the woman is the affected party whose right to know is least often considered.
questions.explanation: 'That is a better instinct than either choice. She is in some sense the affected party.'

questions.prompt: What is Linh's final piece of advice?
options: He should decide in the meeting, not before — people who walk in with a decision defend it; people who walk in with a question hear the answer.
He should record the meeting secretly.
He should pay the back tax preemptively.
He should ask the woman to sign a waiver.
questions.hint: The very last lines of the dialogue.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That the legally cleanest position is always the morally correct one.
That sealing certain information is sometimes defensible ethically, but the executor must carry the defence alone.
That the affected third party is often the person whose interest is least represented in such decisions.
That the form of a report cannot do the moral work of disclosure decisions for the person who must sign it.
questions.explanation: Linh argues precisely against this collapse: legal cleanliness ≠ moral correctness; she identifies cases where they diverge.
questions.hint: Look at Linh's framing of the 10% of cases.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best captures Linh's framing of the choice?
options: 'Whichever choice you make is a moral one, and the form of the report cannot do that work for you.'
'In ninety per cent of cases, I advise full disclosure.'
'The estate will probably owe back tax with interest.'
'It is the only day the office is quiet.'
questions.explanation: This is the dialogue's central ethical claim.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that names the moral burden directly.

cloze.template:
Bao: Thank you for coming on a Saturday.
Linh: It is the only quiet day. Where would you like to begin?
Bao: With the question that is keeping me up. Is the {{b1}} ready?
Linh: It is. Three hundred and fourteen pages. You can have it on Monday. Before you do, decide what you want from me.
Bao: I want the truth. I am the {{b2}}.
Linh: You will get the truth either way. There is a version that contains everything I have found, and a version that contains everything I am required by law and by professional {{b3}} to disclose. They are not the same document. The difference is what I am asking you about.
Bao: Is the difference large?
Linh: It is small in pages and large in {{b4}}. There are four entries, between 1991 and 2003, that suggest your uncle helped a former employee through a financial difficulty in a way the tax authority might still treat as undeclared {{b5}}. He is dead. He paid tax on most of his life. The total amount is not large. The employee, who is now sixty-eight, lives in the apartment your uncle helped her buy. She raised two children there.
Bao: Why do I have to know this?
Linh: Because you are the executor, and because in three months you will be asked, under {{b6}}, whether the financial picture I have presented is complete. If the omission stays, you will, in a strict reading, not have answered honestly.
Bao: What is the harm if it goes in?
Linh: The estate will probably owe back tax with interest. The woman in the apartment may be {{b7}} to testify about what your uncle paid her and why. Whatever she has built around the apartment becomes part of a public record. Your uncle's name appears in a paragraph that summarises his life as a tax case.
Bao: And if it stays out?
Linh: Then you will sign a document that omits something {{b8}}. The omission is small. The omission is also a lie, in a technical sense. If anyone ever audits the audit, both of our reputations are damaged, and yours legally as well.
Bao: What do you usually advise?
Linh: In {{b9}} per cent of cases, I advise full disclosure. It is the cleanest position. The remaining ten per cent are like this one. They are cases where disclosure produces a real harm to a {{b10}} party that the disclosure itself does not remedy. The state collects back tax that does not bring your uncle back; an old woman loses peace; nobody is repaired.
Bao: So you are saying I should bury the four entries.
Linh: No. I am saying that whichever choice you make is a {{b11}} one, and that the form of the report cannot do that work for you. Most clients in your position want me to make the choice. I do not, on principle. I will not put my name on a sealed version unless you have understood, in writing, what is sealed and what your duties as executor are.
Bao: May I ask what you would do, if you were me?
Linh: You may, but I cannot answer cleanly. I will tell you what I have learned. Full disclosure is almost always defensible and almost always proportionate. The harder position is defending non-disclosure when the harm is small, distant, and {{b12}}, and the third party cannot defend themselves. There is a coherent ethical position for that choice. It is not the one the law assumes you will take. It is the position you have to be ready to defend, by yourself, for the rest of your life, if it is ever opened.
Bao: What do most clients choose?
Linh: Full disclosure. Mostly because they want to {{b13}}. A small minority bury, and of those, a smaller minority can defend the choice ten years later. The ones who can defend it later usually insisted, at the time, on understanding exactly what they were burying and whom they were protecting. They did not pretend they had no choice. They chose, and they remembered the choosing.
Bao: Linh, I think I want to talk to the woman in the apartment first.
Linh: That is a better instinct than either choice. She is in some sense the affected party. She is also the person whose right to {{b14}} is least often considered.
Bao: Will you be at the meeting?
Linh: If you want me there. I will not speak. But I will witness what you tell her and what you ask of her. That keeps me on the right side of the {{b15}}. And it makes whatever you decide afterwards harder to forget.
Bao: Set it up for next week.
Linh: I will. One last thing. Whatever you decide, you should decide in the meeting, not {{b16}} it. People who walk in with a decision usually defend it. People who walk in with a question usually hear the answer.

blanks.explanation: Bao asks whether the report is ready.
He is the executor of the estate.
Small in pages, large in consequence.
He will be asked under oath.
In ninety per cent of cases.
Whichever choice is a moral one.
Mostly because they want to sleep.
On the right side of the line.
Decide in the meeting, not before it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Linh says that the few people who can defend a 'burial' ten years later are the ones who refused to pretend they had no choice — they chose, and they remembered the choosing. Is there a decision in your own life where you took an easier path while telling yourself you had no choice? What would change if you instead said clearly: 'I had a choice, and this is what I chose, and this is who I was protecting'?
